title: US Catholic parish-closure reversals & successful Vatican appeals — verified database (Phase 2)
method: Multi-agent workflow: 8 enumerators → 28 case files (web-researched, Fable) → 3-vote adversarial verification (Opus, skeptic-primed) → hand-assembled from journal after the run hit session/credit limits mid-verification. All 64 completed verification votes returned refuted=false (zero refutations). Cases whose verification did not complete are labeled case_filed_pending.

database.ethnicIdentity: none — founded July 1, 1908 as a territorial (not national/ethnic) parish; a 102-year-old landmark church at Detroit and Granger Avenues serving ~1,400 families
database.closureContext: Closure announced March 2009 as part of Bishop Richard Lennon's 2009-2010 diocese-wide reconfiguration, which closed 50 churches across the eight-county diocese citing demographic change, priest shortage, and finances. Bishop Lennon celebrated the formal closing Mass at St. James on June 26, 2010; Fr. Weigand said the parish's last Mass and locked the bronze doors June 27, 2010. The city of Lakewood had designated the church a historic landmark shortly before closure.
database.communityResponse: "Save Saint James," incorporated as Friends of Saint James, Inc. (FOSJ), led the fight: weekly rosary vigils in the church courtyard, prayer services on the church steps (including two Christmas Eve services), hundreds of letters to the Vatican, fundraising (yard signs, t-shirts, calendars, dinners), and community outreach at festivals and parades. Christine LaSalvia authored the canonical appeal; the group hired two Rome-based canon lawyers and Boston activist Peter Borré of the Council of Parishes. Ward 1 councilman Kevin Butler sponsored the city landmark-protection legislation for the building.
database.civilLitigation: None reported. Building protection was pursued through City of Lakewood historic-landmark designation (municipal legislation), not lawsuits.
database.appealRoute: Parishioners' canonical recourse against Bishop Lennon's suppression/closure decrees to the Congregation for the Clergy (appeal written by Christine LaSalvia with two Rome canonists and Peter Borré assisting). The Congregation upheld the appeal in decrees made public March 7-9, 2012 and formally received by the diocese March 14, 2012. Bishop Lennon announced April 17, 2012 — 35 days into his 60-day window — "I will not appeal the decrees to the Apostolic Signatura," so the case never went to the Signatura.
database.outcomeDetail: The Vatican's Congregation for the Clergy upheld the St. James parishioners' appeal and voided Bishop Richard Lennon's decrees suppressing the parish and closing the church, finding he had failed to follow canon law — sources cite both substantive and procedural defects in the closure process. St. James was one of roughly a dozen Cleveland parishes (press initially reported 13, diocese reopened 12) whose closures were overturned in the decrees announced March 7-9, 2012 and received by the diocese March 14, 2012. Lennon announced April 17, 2012 that he would not appeal to the Apostolic Signatura and would restore the parishes. Rev. Joseph G. Workman was appointed and celebrated Mass July 23, 2012, and the church reopened with a Mass of ~1,400 attendees on July 25, 2012, the feast of St. James — parish restored and still operating (now sharing a pastor with St. Clement Parish).
database.status2026Note: reopened 12) whose closures were overturned in the decrees announced March 7-9, 2012 and received by the diocese March 1

database.ethnicIdentity: Slovak (founded May 3, 1903 as the first Slovak Roman Catholic parish on Cleveland's West Side, in the Tremont neighborhood)
database.closureContext: Closed in May 2010 as part of Bishop Richard Lennon's diocese-wide downsizing, which shuttered 50 churches over roughly 15 months in 2009-2010, citing low attendance, priest shortages, and budget problems; more than half the closed parishes were ethnic (largely Eastern European) congregations. St. Wendelin was financially solvent at closure, holding about $1.2 million in its accounts per figures compiled by the lay group Endangered Catholics.
database.communityResponse: Parishioners fought the May 2010 closure and filed a canonical appeal to Rome, one of 12 Cleveland parishioner groups whose coordinated appeals were assisted by Peter Borre of the Boston-based Council of Parishes. Diocese-wide lay resistance included Endangered Catholics (which compiled parish financial data showing St. Wendelin held $1.2M) and Code Purple (founded by Nancy McGrath), with weekly prayer vigils outside closed churches, marches on the Cathedral of St. John the Evangelist, and protest signs dubbing the diocese 'Lennongrad'. After reopening, the parish and neighborhood grew, with a parish prayer garden completed in 2017.
database.civilLitigation: None specific to St. Wendelin found. Diocese-wide context: Code Purple founder Nancy McGrath sued Bishop Lennon and the diocese challenging their authority over the closures, and the diocese countersued McGrath for trespassing.
database.appealRoute: Hierarchical recourse from Bishop Lennon's closure decree to the Congregation for the Clergy, which ruled in favor of all 12 appellant parish groups (announced March 7-8, 2012; official decrees received by Lennon March 14, 2012). Grounds: Lennon failed to follow canonical procedure for closing parishes — e.g., neglecting to consult clerical advisers and failing to issue proper formal decrees. Lennon had 60 days to appeal to the Apostolic Signatura; on April 17, 2012 he announced 'I will not appeal the decrees to the Apostolic Signatura', saying it was 'time for peace and unity in the Diocese of Cleveland', and implemented the reopenings.
database.outcomeDetail: The Vatican's Congregation for the Clergy ruled that Bishop Richard Lennon's 2010 closing of St. Wendelin (one of 12 appealed Cleveland-diocese closures, initially reported as 13) was procedurally invalid under canon law — he had failed to follow required canonical procedure, including consulting clerical advisers and issuing proper formal decrees. The decision was announced March 7, 2012, with official decrees received by Lennon on March 14, 2012. Lennon declined to appeal to the Apostolic Signatura (announced April 17, 2012), making the reversal final; St. Wendelin reopened with Mass on July 29, 2012 — nine months short of its 110th anniversary — under Rev. Robert Kropac, and remains an active parish in the Diocese of Cleveland directory as of 2026.
database.status2026Note: remains an active parish in the Diocese of Cleveland directory as of 2026

database.ethnicIdentity: German — founded 1853 by Fr. John H. Luhr for Cleveland's German immigrant Catholics (1859 church at Superior Ave. and E. 17th St.); by the closure era it functioned as a non-ethnic downtown parish.
database.closureContext: Diocese-wide downsizing under Bishop Richard Lennon: a May 2007 directive ordered parish clusters to plan to merge or close at least 45 of 231 parishes, and about 50 churches were closed 2009-2010 (closures announced March 2009), citing demographic shifts, priest shortage, and finances. St. Peter, the diocese's oldest continuously operating parish, was slated for merger with the nearby Cathedral of St. John and closed in April 2010; pastor Fr. Robert J. Marrone presided at the final liturgy on Easter Sunday, April 4, 2010, then locked the church doors.
database.communityResponse: Two-track response. (1) A group of original parishioners filed canonical recourse to Rome against the closure, part of a coordinated wave of Cleveland appeals (activist Peter Borré of the Council of Parishes helped announce the eventual Vatican rulings). (2) Most of the active congregation refused to disperse: they formed a nonprofit corporation, worshipped at Cleveland State University for about four months, and from August 2010 met as the independent "Community of St. Peter" (~300 members) in a refurbished warehouse at 7100 Euclid Ave., with Fr. Marrone celebrating weekly Mass outside diocesan authority. After the parish's 2012 restoration the breakaway community declined to return; Lennon declared Marrone excommunicated latae sententiae for schism (canon 1364 §1) on March 4, 2013, and the Community of St. Peter continues today as an independent congregation.
database.civilLitigation: None reported for St. Peter; the fight was waged through canonical channels and the independent breakaway community.
database.appealRoute: Parishioners' hierarchical recourse against Bishop Lennon's suppression/closure went to the Congregation for the Clergy (filed 2010). In decrees publicly reported March 7-8, 2012 and officially received by the diocese March 14, 2012, the Congregation sustained St. Peter's appeal along with 12 other Cleveland parishes. Lennon had 60 days to appeal to the Apostolic Signatura; on April 17, 2012 he announced he would not appeal ("it is time for peace and unity in the Diocese of Cleveland"), making the decrees final.
database.protocolNumbers: None reported for St. Peter; a quoted decree fragment in the companion St. Patrick (West Park) case referenced "St. Patrick Parish 515".
database.outcomeDetail: The Vatican's Congregation for the Clergy voided Bishop Richard Lennon's April 2010 closure of St. Peter — one of 13 Cleveland parish closings it reversed in March 2012 — ruling that Lennon erred both procedurally and substantively under canon law (defective consultation of his priests' council and failure to issue proper decrees), and ordering the parishes restored and the churches reopened for worship. Lennon declined to appeal to the Apostolic Signatura (announced April 17, 2012), appointed Fr. Robert J. Kropac pastor on July 10, 2012, and St. Peter reopened with Mass on September 9, 2012; it remains an open diocesan parish. The reversal restored the canonical parish, while the breakaway Community of St. Peter formed during the closure stayed independent under Fr. Robert Marrone, whom Lennon declared excommunicated for schism on March 4, 2013.

database.parish: St. Adalbert (St. Adalbert / Our Lady of the Blessed Sacrament Parish)
database.ethnicIdentity: Czech (Bohemian) national parish at founding (est. January 1, 1883 by Bishop Richard Gilmour); since the February 1961 merger that moved Our Lady of the Blessed Sacrament (est. April 11, 1922 as Ohio's first personal parish founded by and for African American Catholics) into the St. Adalbert church, it has been Cleveland's historic Black Catholic parish
database.closureContext: Part of Bishop Richard Lennon's 2009-2010 downsizing of the Diocese of Cleveland (announced March 2009; ~50-52 parishes closed or merged across eight counties, citing demographic shifts, priest shortage, and finances). After the Vibrant Parish Life clustering of St. Adalbert with St. Agnes-Our Lady of Fatima and St. Aloysius-St. Agatha, Lennon ordered St. Adalbert suppressed outright rather than merged; the church at 2347 E. 83rd St. closed June 6, 2010, displacing 200+ parishioners, with the diocese citing declining membership and maintenance costs. The building sat shuttered but otherwise untouched for two years (the parish school stayed open).
database.communityResponse: Nearly two-year canonical appeal campaign rather than occupation: parishioners led by parish historian Phillis Clipps filed canonical recourse and waged a sustained letter-writing effort to Rome (apostolic nuncio Cardinal Pietro Sambi personally acknowledged receiving their materials). The appeal was part of a coordinated 13-church Cleveland effort assisted by Boston-based Council of Parishes activist Peter Borre; the local coalition Endangered Catholics held weekly vigil-light prayer protests outside St. John the Evangelist Cathedral against the closures and a May 2010 rally at Cleveland City Hall with City Council support. After the reversal, members returned to scrub every inch of the church for the August 2012 reopening.
database.civilLitigation: None reported for St. Adalbert; the parishioners' fight was pursued entirely through canonical channels.
database.appealRoute: Parishioners' recourse against Bishop Lennon's suppression/closure decree to the Congregation for the Clergy (filed 2010, part of 13 coordinated Cleveland-area appeals; Peter Borre of the Council of Parishes assisted). The Congregation ruled for the parishioners in decrees dated March 1, 2012, received by the diocese March 14, 2012. Lennon had 60 days to appeal to the Apostolic Signatura but announced on April 17, 2012 that he would not, making the reversal final.
database.protocolNumbers: Not reported publicly for St. Adalbert; press coverage described the March 1, 2012 Congregation for the Clergy decrees without citing protocol numbers.
database.outcomeDetail: The Vatican's Congregation for the Clergy, in decrees dated March 1, 2012, upheld the appeals of all 13 closed Cleveland-area churches that had appealed — St. Adalbert among them (Plain Dealer, March 10, 2012) — finding Bishop Richard Lennon failed to follow canon law both procedurally and substantively ('de procedendo and de decernendo', e.g., defective consultation such as failure to properly consult the presbyteral council and inadequate canonical cause), voiding his closure decrees and ordering the parishes restored with the churches reopened for worship. On April 17, 2012 Lennon announced he would not appeal to the Apostolic Signatura and would reopen the 12 affected parishes, saying it was 'time for peace and unity in the Diocese of Cleveland.' Fr. Gary Chmura was appointed pastor in July 2012 and St. Adalbert reopened for Mass in August 2012; the parish celebrated its OLBS centennial in April 2022 and remains open.

database.ethnicIdentity: Polish — canonically designated "a personal parish for those Catholics of the Latin Rite of Polish descent" under canon 518; founded 1891 by Polish immigrants. (St. Casimir is the patron saint of both Poland and Lithuania, but this parish is Polish, not Lithuanian.)
database.closureContext: Part of Bishop Richard Lennon's 2009–2010 diocese-wide downsizing that closed or merged roughly 50 parishes (52 church buildings), citing demographic shifts, priest shortage, and finances. Lennon consulted the Presbyteral Council on St. Casimir on February 3, 2009, announced suppression by letter of March 12, 2009 (declaring the church would no longer be used for worship and would be fenced off), and presided over the final closing Mass on November 8, 2009 — protesters called it a "Mass of Eviction," held with plainclothes police inside the church.
database.communityResponse: Beginning November 15, 2009, parishioners held street prayer vigils outside the locked, fenced church every Sunday at 11:30 a.m. for 139 consecutive Sundays (~2.7 years; some accounts say 140 — the candidate claim's "135 weeks" is slightly understated), praying in Polish and English under the motto "Nie bójcie się" ("Do not be afraid"); vigil leaders included Joe Feckanin, Stan Zadnik, and Wojtek Fleszar. Parishioners also ran letter-writing campaigns to Rome (including January 2012), held a May 2010 rally at Cleveland City Hall, and coordinated with Peter Borre of the Boston-based Council of Parishes, which supported the Cleveland appeals; the diocesan-level lay coalition Endangered Catholics also agitated over the closures.
database.civilLitigation: None found for St. Casimir. The challenge proceeded entirely through canonical channels; parishioners held vigils on the public street rather than occupying the building, and no civil suit over the closure is reported in the sources.
database.appealRoute: Full canonical route: (1) written requests to Bishop Lennon to amend his decree per canon 1734, March 23 and 26, 2009; (2) Lennon rejected both, April 14 and 23, 2009; (3) hierarchical recourse to the Congregation for the Clergy filed April 23 and May 2, 2009, petitioning that Lennon's decree be declared null and void; (4) Congregation for the Clergy combined decree of March 1, 2012 upheld the recourse on both procedural and substantive grounds; (5) Lennon, notified March 14, 2012, had 60 days to take recourse to the Apostolic Signatura under Antiqua Ordinatione but announced on April 17, 2012 that he would not appeal ("it is time for peace and unity in the Diocese of Cleveland"), rendering the decrees final.
database.protocolNumbers: Congregation for the Clergy Decree Prot. N. 20120457, dated March 1, 2012, signed by Cardinal Mauro Piacenza (Prefect) and Titular Archbishop Celso Morga Iruzubieta (Secretary) — the decree specific to the combined St. Casimir appeals.
database.outcomeDetail: The Vatican's Congregation for the Clergy (Decree Prot. N. 20120457, March 1, 2012, signed by Cardinal Piacenza) declared Bishop Lennon's suppression of St. Casimir parish AND his closure of the church invalid, finding he violated canon law: defective consultation of the Presbyteral Council (cann. 515§2, 127§2), absence of a properly motivated formal decree (can. 51), and de facto relegation of the church to profane use without following canon 1222§2 procedures. St. Casimir was one of 12 Cleveland parishes restored (14 had appealed; 2 were denied) — initial press on March 7-9, 2012 reported 13 winners, but the diocese reopened only 12, and St. Margaret Mary of South Euclid was not among them (NCR reported its status in question/appeal turned down; it was later merged). Lennon announced April 17, 2012 that he would not appeal to the Apostolic Signatura and would reopen all 12. St. Casimir reopened with more than 1,000 people (parish says over 1,200) at the first Mass on July 15, 2012, celebrated by newly appointed pastor Fr. Eric Orzech; the parish remains active today, listed as "St. Casimir (Polish)" in the Diocese of Cleveland directory with Fr. Orzech still pastor and Sunday Mass at 11:30 a.m. — the same hour as the vigils.

database.ethnicIdentity: Polish (national parish founded 1905 for Polish immigrants in Brooklyn Centre, the area known in Polish as "na Barbarowie"; parish bulletin carried Polish-language sections; interior honors Polish saints Faustina Kowalska and Maximilian Kolbe)
database.closureContext: Part of Bishop Richard Lennon's 2009-10 diocese-wide downsizing that closed ~50 of the Diocese of Cleveland's parishes, citing demographic shifts, priest shortage, and finances; more than half the closed parishes were ethnic (nine Polish). St. Barbara was notified by Lennon's letter of March 14, 2009; Lennon himself celebrated the final Mass on May 9, 2010, and the parish was suppressed.
database.communityResponse: Parishioners mounted what local sources call a "relentless fight": an organized parishioner group filed hierarchical recourse against the suppression to the Congregation for the Clergy, one of about a dozen Cleveland-area appellant groups whose Roman appeals were coordinated with Boston-based Council of Parishes activist Peter Borre and canon lawyers in Rome. Diocese-wide lay opposition groups Code Purple (Nancy McGrath) and Endangered Catholics organized marches on the cathedral and petitions against the closures, which critics said targeted ethnic parishes. No St. Barbara-specific vigil or occupation is documented (the famous 139-Sunday vigils were at sister Polish parish St. Casimir).
database.civilLitigation: None documented for St. Barbara specifically; its fight was canonical. (Diocese-wide, Code Purple founder Nancy McGrath filed a civil lawsuit over other closures.)
database.appealRoute: Parishioner hierarchical recourse against Bishop Lennon's suppression decree, filed 2010 to the Congregation for the Clergy, which UPHELD the recourse: decision announced March 7, 2012, formal decrees received by Lennon March 14, 2012, covering St. Barbara and the other Cleveland appellant parishes (13 churches per initial reports; 12 parish decrees per the diocese). Lennon had 60 days to appeal to the Apostolic Signatura; on April 17, 2012 (day 35) he announced "I will not appeal the decrees to the Apostolic Signatura," making the reversal final. No appeal to the Signatura occurred.
database.protocolNumbers: None reported in press coverage reviewed.
database.outcomeDetail: The Vatican's Congregation for the Clergy upheld St. Barbara parishioners' canonical appeal and voided Bishop Richard Lennon's decrees suppressing the parish and closing its church, ruling he had erred both procedurally and substantively under canon law — including failure to properly consult the presbyteral council as required for validity — and ordering the parish restored and the church returned to worship (decision announced March 7, 2012; decrees received March 14, 2012). Lennon announced April 17, 2012 he would not appeal to the Apostolic Signatura and would implement the decrees. The parish was re-established with Fr. Joseph Hilinski appointed pastor effective July 16, 2012 — the church's official reopening date, ~26 months after closure — and the first Mass was celebrated July 22, 2012. The reversal proved durable: St. Barbara remains an active parish in the diocesan directory and celebrated its 120th anniversary in January 2026.
database.status2026Note: remains an active parish in the diocesan directory and celebrated its 120th anniversary in January 2026

database.ethnicIdentity: Hungarian — national/ethnic parish founded 1904 to serve Hungarian immigrants on Cleveland's West Side (Ohio City, adjacent to the West Side Market); Hungarian-language parish with Hungarian-born pastor Fr. Sandor Siklodi
database.closureContext: Part of Bishop Richard Lennon's 2009-2010 diocesan reconfiguration that closed or merged roughly 50 of the eight-county diocese's parishes, citing demographic shifts, priest shortage, and finances. St. Emeric was slated for suppression in the March 2009 announcement; after 15 months of parishioner resistance it was the last of the scheduled closings, with Lennon himself presiding at the June 30, 2010 final Mass. The Congregation for the Clergy later found the diocese had used an informal March 12, 2009 letter rather than formal canonical decrees, and had conflated parish suppression with relegation of the church building to profane use.
database.communityResponse: Fifteen months of continuing appeals and resistance between the closure announcement and the June 2010 final Mass. Parishioner Miklós Peller served as procurator for the canonical appeal (with Ildikó Peller), filed after Lennon's March 2009 announcement; the Hungarian American Coalition backed the effort, with Coalition treasurer Zsolt Szekeres and Ambassador Georg Habsburg personally hand-delivering St. Emeric's appeal to the Vatican in spring 2010. During the 2+ years of closure a prayer group led by János Juhász held the community together, Cleveland's Hungarian Scouts (about 100 attended the reopening) stayed engaged, and displaced parishioners were hosted at St. Colman (Fr. Robert Begin, who concelebrated the reopening Mass). The appeal also demanded reinstatement of 25-year pastor Fr. Sandor Siklodi, which Peller's lobbying eventually won — the only ousted pastor among the appealing parishes returned to his own pulpit.
database.civilLitigation: None reported for St. Emeric — the campaign was canonical and diplomatic (Vatican recourse, Hungarian-American advocacy), not civil court litigation.
database.appealRoute: Bishop Lennon's suppression/closure (announced March 2009, executed June 30, 2010) → hierarchical recourse by parishioners to the Congregation for the Clergy (procurator Miklós Peller; appeal hand-delivered to the Vatican spring 2010) → Congregation decrees dated March 1, 2012 upheld the recourse on both the parish suppression (canon 515 §2 — defective decree, failure to properly consult the presbyteral council) and the church closure (canon 1222 §2 — never lawfully relegated to profane use). No Apostolic Signatura stage: Lennon announced April 17, 2012, 'I will not appeal the decrees to the Apostolic Signatura,' making the decrees final. Fourteen Cleveland parishes had appealed; twelve were upheld, two denied (press counts varied 11-13).
database.protocolNumbers: Prot. N. 20120461 (Congregation for the Clergy decree re St. Emeric, dated March 1, 2012), per the decree table compiled in the St. Casimir Church (Cleveland) Wikipedia article; companion Cleveland decrees include Prot. N. 20120457 (St. Casimir). Single-source detail — not independently confirmed.
database.outcomeDetail: The Vatican's Congregation for the Clergy, in decrees dated March 1, 2012 (reported March 7; official notice received by the diocese March 14), upheld St. Emeric parishioners' recourse and voided Bishop Richard Lennon's suppression: the Congregation ruled the petition 'does have canonical basis in law and in fact,' finding Lennon violated canon 515 §2 in suppressing the parish (no proper formal decree, inadequate consultation of the presbyteral council) and that the church 'has not been lawfully and validly relegated' to profane use under canon 1222 §2. Reported as the Congregation's first actual reversal of parish closings in about two decades, covering 12 upheld Cleveland appeals. Lennon announced April 17, 2012 he would not appeal to the Apostolic Signatura and would reopen the churches. St. Emeric was the last of the restored churches to reopen: Fr. Sandor Siklodi was reappointed pastor October 19, 2012 (per the Diocese of Cleveland), and the reopening Mass on November 4, 2012 — St. Emeric's feast day and the anniversary of the Soviet crushing of the 1956 Hungarian Revolution — drew roughly 500-600 people. The parish remained open thereafter; a July 2023 decree by Bishop Edward Malesic merged St. Elizabeth of Hungary with St. Emeric, with St. Emeric retaining parish status.

database.ethnicIdentity: Irish heritage — founded March 17, 1848 by Irish (and some German) farm families in Rockport Township; the historic parish of Cleveland's heavily Irish West Park neighborhood. A territorial parish, not a formally erected national/ethnic parish.
database.closureDate: 2010-05-30 (church locked after final services; closure ordered May 2009)
database.closureContext: Part of Bishop Richard Lennon's diocese-wide downsizing announced March 2009 (52 parishes cut — 29 closed, 41 merged — citing priest shortage, suburban migration, and parish finances, effective by June 30, 2010). St. Patrick, a 1,100-family parish dating to 1848 at Rocky River Dr. and Puritas Ave., was ordered in May 2009 to close and merge with Ascension and Annunciation parishes; the church was locked May 30, 2010.
database.communityResponse: Parishioners organized quickly and raised about $13,000 for a sustained campaign — mailings, T-shirts, and billboards — and hired canon lawyers in Rome to press their appeal. The parish coordinated with the Boston-based Council of Parishes (Peter Borré), which supported the 13 appellant Cleveland-area parishes; Cleveland-wide protest groups (Code Purple, Endangered Catholics) kept public pressure on the diocese. In summer 2011 the Congregation for the Clergy, reviewing the diocese's conduct, extended St. Patrick's appeal to March 2012 rather than dismissing it.
database.civilLitigation: None reported for St. Patrick West Park — its fight was entirely canonical. (Separately, Code Purple leader Nancy McGrath sued the Cleveland diocese challenging the bishop's civil-law authority to close churches without parishioner consent, but that suit did not involve this parish.)
database.appealRoute: Recourse against Bishop Lennon's decrees (diocesan-level appeals rejected) → hierarchical recourse to the Congregation for the Clergy, pursued by Rome-based canon lawyers hired by parishioners, with Council of Parishes support; appeal extended in summer 2011 and decided in the parish's favor in March 2012. The decree voided both the canon 515 suppression of the parish and the canon 1222 closing of the church, upholding the petition 'both de procedendo and de decernendo' (on procedure and on the merits). Bishop Lennon had 60 days to appeal to the Apostolic Signatura but announced April 17, 2012: 'For peace and unity in the Diocese of Cleveland, I will not appeal.'
database.protocolNumbers: Not reported in available sources. The Congregation for the Clergy decrees (received by the diocese March 14, 2012) were leaked/quoted in press — decree language for the sister parishes reads that the petition 'does have canonical basis in law and in fact, and so is upheld both de procedendo and de decernendo,' with the Bishop of Cleveland 'instructed to enact the implications of this Decree' — but no Prot. N. was published.
database.outcomeDate: 2012-03 (decrees publicly reported March 7-8, 2012; officially received by diocese March 14, 2012); church reopened July 2012
database.outcomeDetail: The Vatican's Congregation for the Clergy upheld the parishioners' canonical recourse against Bishop Richard Lennon, voiding both the canon 515 suppression of St. Patrick Parish and the canon 1222 closing/relegation of its church, on grounds that Lennon failed to follow canon law and proper procedure (including defective consultation of the presbyteral council) — the petition was upheld 'both de procedendo and de decernendo.' St. Patrick was one of 13 appellant parishes in the Cleveland diocese whose closures the Congregation overturned in March 2012 — the largest mass reversal of parish closings ever issued by Rome against a US diocese. On April 17, 2012 Lennon announced he would not appeal to the Apostolic Signatura and would reopen the churches (12 reopened, including St. Patrick). The parish was restored and reopened its doors to the West Park community in July 2012; it remains an active diocesan parish today and celebrated its 175th anniversary in 2023-24.
database.status2026Note: remains an active diocesan parish today and celebrated its 175th anniversary in 2023-24

database.ethnicIdentity: none/unknown (territorial parish)
database.closureContext: Closure announced in May 2004 as part of Archbishop Sean P. O'Malley's archdiocese-wide 2004 reconfiguration that targeted roughly 80 parishes for suppression, citing priest shortages, declining attendance, and financial strain after the sex-abuse crisis. St. Albert's final Mass was celebrated by Fr. Ronald Coyne on Aug. 29/Sept. 1, 2004, and the parish was suppressed effective Sept. 1, 2004 — despite full pews and healthy collections, which parishioners cited as evidence the closure was unjustified.
database.communityResponse: Parishioners began a round-the-clock occupation ("perpetual vigil") of the church on Sept. 1, 2004, that lasted approximately 10 months until July 2, 2005 — hundreds of volunteers in organized shifts, twice-daily prayer services, rosaries, lay-led Communion services with hosts consecrated by a sympathetic priest, and religious education. It was the first and most prominent of the Boston vigils and inspired at least eight other occupied parishes. The vigil continued after the March 2005 reopening announcement until conditions (funds, records, services restored) were met.
database.civilLitigation: Parishioners (with other Boston vigil parishes) pursued civil claims arguing the archdiocese held parish property as trustee for parishioners rather than as outright owner; the reversal, however, came administratively from the archbishop, not from any court order.
database.appealRoute: Reversal achieved at the diocesan level: O'Malley's lay-dominated review commission (the Meade-Eisner Commission), created in December 2004 to re-examine reconfiguration decisions, recommended reopening; O'Malley announced the decision March 31, 2005 and formally revoked the suppression decree in June 2005. No recourse to the Congregation for the Clergy or Apostolic Signatura was needed for this parish, and no protocol numbers were reported.
database.outcomeDetail: Archbishop Sean P. O'Malley himself revoked his own 2004 suppression decree: after his lay Meade-Eisner review commission recommended reopening, he announced on March 31, 2005 that St. Albert the Great would remain open, and formally signed the decree revoking the closure in mid-June 2005 (reported June 13-14, 2005), declaring the parish "remains open as a full parish." Weekly Masses resumed and the church reopened July 2, 2005, with Fr. Laurence J. Borges appointed pastor. The parish remains an active Roman Catholic parish today (sharing clergy with St. Francis Xavier, Weymouth) — the flagship reversal of Boston's 2004 reconfiguration.

database.ethnicIdentity: none/unknown
database.closureDate: 2004 (first closure order, archdiocesan reconfiguration); announced again Aug. 27, 2020 with closing set for Oct. 3, 2020
database.closureContext: First threat: 2004 archdiocese-wide reconfiguration under Archbishop Sean O'Malley (post-abuse-crisis parish closures); St. Anselm was ordered closed. Second threat: in May 2020 the archdiocese merged St. Anselm with St. Bridget (Maynard) and Our Lady of Fatima (Sudbury) into the Maynard/Sudbury Catholic Collaborative, then on Aug. 27, 2020 announced St. Anselm would close Oct. 3, 2020, citing low Mass attendance and financial considerations ("hemorrhaging funds"); services were suspended ahead of the planned closing. Parishioners countered that the community was financially independent, debt-free, and had "never taken a dime from the archdiocese."
database.communityResponse: 2004: round-the-clock occupation vigil lasting nearly two years, led by parish council chair Cynthia Deysher, until the archdiocese relented. 2020: a second 24/7 vigil begun in early September 2020 (made public Sept. 10 after archdiocese officials were denied entry), again organized by lay leaders Cynthia Deysher and Jamie Hanson of the Lay Leadership Council; Deysher personally appealed to Cardinal O'Malley.
database.civilLitigation: None reported in any source for either episode.
database.appealRoute: No formal canonical appeal to the Congregation/Dicastery for the Clergy or Apostolic Signatura is documented for St. Anselm in either episode. Both reversals were obtained at the diocesan level: the 2004-2006 outcome through sustained vigil pressure during the archdiocese's reconfiguration review, and the 2020 outcome through a direct personal appeal by lay leader Cynthia Deysher to Cardinal Sean O'Malley. No protocol numbers reported.
database.outcomeDate: 2020-10-01 (second reversal); earlier reversal 2005 (reopened as worship site) and 2006 (erected as rectorate)
database.outcomeDetail: Two distinct reversals by the Archdiocese of Boston. First: after the 2004 closure order and a nearly two-year occupation vigil, the archdiocese relented, reopening St. Anselm in 2005 as a chapel/worship site of St. George Parish (Framingham); in 2006 Cardinal Sean O'Malley erected it as a rectorate (reported as the first rectorate in the United States), removing territorial parish status but keeping the church open for regular worship. Second: after the Aug. 27, 2020 announcement that the church would close Oct. 3, 2020, and a renewed 24/7 vigil, Cardinal O'Malley personally reversed the closure on Oct. 1, 2020 — two days before the closing date and before any suppression/relegation decree took effect — telling lay leader Cynthia Deysher the church could remain open. The Boston Catholic Directory confirms St. Anselm remains active in July 2026 with an administrator, staff, and a full Mass schedule.

database.communityResponse: Parishioners filed a canonical appeal to Rome arguing the parish was self-sustaining, one of about a dozen Cleveland-diocese parish groups that appealed. The appeals were coordinated with help from Peter Borre of the Boston-based Council of Parishes; the Cleveland-area lay group Endangered Catholics publicly campaigned against Lennon's closures (its VP Bob Kloos called the Vatican reversals "the most stunning" in U.S. Catholic history). Round-the-clock vigils occurred at other Cleveland parishes (e.g., St. Casimir) but are not documented at St. John the Baptist itself.

database.protocolNumbers: Not reported in the sources consulted (press referenced the individual March 2012 Congregation for the Clergy decrees, e.g. the "St. James decree," but did not publish protocol numbers).
database.outcomeDetail: The Vatican's Congregation for the Clergy, in decrees reported March 7, 2012, invalidated Bishop Richard Lennon's suppression/closure of 13 Cleveland-diocese parishes including St. John the Baptist, Akron, ruling the closings "not valid" because Lennon failed to follow canon law and procedure (including inadequate consultation of his presbyteral council and defective decrees conflating parish suppression with church closure). Lennon announced April 17, 2012 he would not appeal to the Apostolic Signatura and would reopen 12 parishes; Rev. A. Jonathon Zingales was named pastor of St. John the Baptist effective July 2, 2012, and the parish reopened that summer. The diocesan directory lists it as an active parish today (pastor Fr. Dismas Byarugaba, Sunday Mass 10:00 AM).

Cullen issued a decree of suppression as part of a diocese-wide restructuring that closed roughly 47 parishes; St. Joseph was merged with four other Bethlehem ethnic parishes into the new Incarnation of Our Lord parish, with the church slated for closure. Parishioners noted the parish was financially solvent (~$300,000 in savings) and had even offered to obtain a priest from Murska Sobota, Slovenia.
database.communityResponse: Parishioners organized a canonical appeal (filed with the Congregation for the Clergy July 24, 2008). After the 2011 victory they continued annual feast-day Masses and funerals, and later formed the Society of St. Joseph of Bethlehem (SSJB), a nonprofit created solely to buy and maintain the church; when the diocese relisted the building for sale in 2023, the James Stocklas Family Trust donated the full $175,000 purchase price and SSJB completed the purchase (announced March 2024), keeping it a Catholic chapel.
database.civilLitigation: None reported.
database.appealRoute: Recourse from the bishop's suppression decree to the Congregation for the Clergy, filed July 24, 2008; decided by decree of January 5, 2011. Bishop John Barres then filed his own appeal seeking to reverse the Congregation's January 5, 2011 decree, but the diocese withdrew that appeal and instead set operating conditions (Mass on the feast of St. Joseph and funeral Masses for former parishioners). No Apostolic Signatura stage; no protocol numbers reported in available sources.
database.outcomeDetail: The Congregation for the Clergy, by decree dated January 5, 2011, partially reversed Bishop Cullen's May 29, 2008 action: it upheld the suppression/merger of the parish but ruled that the church building "must remain open to allow the faithful a place for divine worship" — part of the Congregation's 2011 line of Allentown rulings holding that a bishop may not close a church edifice merely as a consequence of a parish merger absent the grave cause canon law requires. The diocese, after withdrawing its counter-appeal, kept the church open for an annual feast-day Mass and funerals. The building's Catholic status was permanently secured in 2023-2024 when the Society of St. Joseph of Bethlehem purchased it from the diocese for $175,000; it remains a Catholic chapel under diocesan jurisdiction with two required annual Masses (March 19 feast day and October 28 consecration anniversary). Note: this is a church-closure reversal, not a restoration of the parish itself — the merger into Incarnation of Our Lord stood.

database.closureContext: Part of Bishop Edward P. Cullen's 2008 diocesan-wide consolidation that closed dozens of churches across the Diocese of Allentown. Effective July 15, 2008, St. Francis of Assisi Parish was suppressed and merged with St. Barbara, St. Mary Star of the Sea (Branchdale), and St. Stanislaus Kostka to form the new St. Matthew the Evangelist Parish, Minersville; the St. Francis church building was closed to worship.
database.communityResponse: Parishioners filed canonical recourse to Rome against the closure; their fight drew AP national coverage (AP photo of St. Francis parishioners standing before the closed church, Jan. 25, 2011). The Allentown appeals (14 pending) were part of the multi-diocese appeal wave coordinated with Boston-based Council of Parishes leader Peter Borre. No vigil or occupation reported for this church.
database.civilLitigation: None reported.
database.appealRoute: Parishioners' hierarchical recourse against the bishop's decree went to the Vatican Congregation for the Clergy, which ruled in the parishioners' favor on the church building in early 2011 (Allentown decrees handed down December 2010-January 2011); the bishop retained but did not exercise the option of further recourse to the Apostolic Signatura. No protocol numbers reported in available sources.
database.outcomeDetail: The Vatican Congregation for the Clergy ruled that the Bishop of Allentown acted correctly de procedendo in suppressing/merging St. Francis of Assisi Parish, but de decernendo failed to establish the 'graves causae' required by Canon 1222 to relegate the church to profane use — i.e., no showing that the church 'cannot be used in any way for divine worship' and cannot be repaired. The parish merger stood, but the church building had to remain available for Catholic worship — the lead 'Minersville ruling' among a set of similar Allentown decrees (St. Joseph Bethlehem, St. Canicus Mahanoy City, Our Lady of Mount Carmel Nesquehoning, Mary Queen of Peace Pottsville, Immaculate Conception St. Clair, St. Michael Tresckow, St. Roch West Bangor). Canon lawyer Nicholas Cafardi called the rulings a landmark for the rights of the faithful. Caveat: the diocese complied minimally (roughly one Mass a year in the building), and in the 2020s the church was finally closed/relegated citing little use, unchallenged because the Lithuanian community had dispersed — but the 2011 decree itself was a genuine Vatican reversal of the church closure that kept the building in sacred use for over a decade.

database.closureContext: Suppressed in the Archdiocese of Boston's 2004 archdiocese-wide parish reconfiguration (begun January 2004), which closed dozens of parishes citing falling attendance, priest shortage, and financial strain in the wake of the clergy sexual-abuse settlement crisis. Infant Jesus-St. Lawrence was listed among the Central Region parish closings; the church was closed in late 2004.
database.communityResponse: Parishioners pursued a sustained canonical appeal campaign coordinated with the Council of Parishes, the lay organization (co-chaired by Peter Borre) formed to oppose the Boston closure process. Infant Jesus-St. Lawrence was one of roughly a dozen suppressed parishes whose parishioners appealed to Rome and one of the eight that carried the appeal through the Apostolic Signatura and its full college of judges. No round-the-clock vigil/occupation at this church was verified (the five vigil churches were other parishes); one unverifiable 404'd source suggested a sit-in but this could not be confirmed.
database.civilLitigation: None found for this parish.
database.appealRoute: Archbishop (Seán P. O'Malley) → Congregation for the Clergy (upheld the archdiocese's suppression, 2006) → Apostolic Signatura (appeal rejected, confirmed by archdiocese June 10, 2008) → full college of judges of the Apostolic Signatura (final rejection May 7, 2010, announced to parish leaders May 17, 2010). Every level upheld the suppression.
database.outcomeDetail: Church reopened, but the parish suppression stood. The Archdiocese of Boston itself (under Archbishop Seán O'Malley, during the 2005 re-evaluation of the reconfiguration) reopened St. Lawrence Church in June 2005 as a second worship site of neighboring St. Mary of the Assumption Parish, Brookline — one of three churches with pending Apostolic Signatura appeals reopened in 2005 as worship sites of neighboring parishes, per both the Boston Pilot and CNS. The canonical appeal to restore the suppressed Infant Jesus-St. Lawrence Parish failed at every Vatican level (Congregation for Clergy 2006; Signatura June 10, 2008; full college May 7, 2010). The building nonetheless returned to and remains in regular Catholic use: the parish today describes itself as 'One parish, two churches,' with Sunday Mass at St. Lawrence, 774 Boylston St., Chestnut Hill. This qualifies under the 'church reopened' criterion, but the reverser was the archdiocese via administrative reconsideration, not the Vatican, and the juridic parish was never restored.

database.ethnicIdentity: Slovak (national parish founded 1905 for St. Clair's Slovak community; the borough's other parishes were German, Irish, Lithuanian, and Polish)
database.closureContext: Part of Bishop Edward Cullen's 2008 diocese-wide consolidation that closed 47 parishes (151 to 104) across the Allentown diocese, driven by population decline in the anthracite coal region and fewer priests. Effective July 15, 2008 the four St. Clair parishes — St. Boniface (German, 1853), St. Mary (Irish, 1864), Immaculate Conception (Slovak, 1905), and St. Casimir (Lithuanian, 1912) — were suppressed and merged into the new St. Clare of Assisi Parish, and Immaculate Conception church was closed to regular worship.
database.communityResponse: Parishioners of Immaculate Conception filed canonical recourse to Rome against both the suppression and the church closure; theirs was one of 14 appeals pending from the Allentown diocese. Contemporaneous coverage (CNS, NCR, Buffalo News, CBS) treated the Allentown parishioner appeals as an organized wave that raised hopes for appellants in Buffalo, Springfield, and New Jersey. No vigils, occupations, or a named advocacy organization specific to Immaculate Conception St. Clair were found in the sources consulted.
database.civilLitigation: None found for this parish.
database.appealRoute: Parishioner recourse against Bishop Cullen's 2008 decrees to the Congregation for the Clergy, which issued its decree in January 2011. No onward appeal to the Apostolic Signatura was needed on the church-closure question (the parishioners won that point); the suppression portion was upheld and no successful Signatura reversal of it is documented. No protocol numbers reported in available press.
database.outcomeDetail: In January 2011 the Vatican's Congregation for the Clergy, ruling on parishioner recourse, upheld the 2008 suppression of Immaculate Conception parish into St. Clare of Assisi Parish but overturned the closure of the church building, finding the bishop had not demonstrated the grave cause canon law (c. 1222) requires to withdraw a church from sacred worship, and ordered the building reopened/retained for worship as determined by the bishop. It was one of seven Allentown churches receiving such split decrees that month (with St. Joseph Bethlehem, St. Canicus Mahanoy City, Our Lady of Mount Carmel Nesquehoning, Mary Queen of Peace Pottsville, St. Michael Tresckow, St. Roch West Bangor). The building was thereafter maintained by St. Clare of Assisi Parish for an annual Mass and funerals of former parishioners. Epilogue: in June 2020 the diocese announced, effective July 1, 2020, closure of the former Immaculate Conception building via a new process citing declining use and maintenance costs — a separate later action that does not negate the 2011 reversal but ended the building's sacred use.

database.closureContext: Under Bishop Michael Fisher's "Road to Renewal" restructuring — driven in large part by tens of millions of dollars owed in Child Victims Act sex-abuse settlements and the diocese's Chapter 11 bankruptcy — the diocese announced in September 2024 that St. Bernadette would merge into Ss. Peter & Paul Parish in Hamburg and be reduced to a secondary worship site. The formal merger decree was issued in November 2024 with implementation scheduled for May 2025; the decree was suspended pending the parish's Vatican appeal. The plan was part of a wave that proposed merging dozens of parishes and closing as many as 70 Western New York churches, roughly 30 of which filed Vatican appeals.
database.communityResponse: Parishioners publicly opposed the merger and filed a canonical appeal (hierarchical recourse) to Rome. The grassroots group Save Our Buffalo Churches, led by Mary Pruski, organized and supported the appeal effort across the diocese; advocates documented the parish's vitality — healthy finances, active sacramental life including baptisms and weddings — as grounds that suppression/merger was unwarranted. In April 2025, St. Bernadette was among 14 Buffalo parishes notified that the Vatican had accepted their cases for full review.
database.civilLitigation: None reported specific to this parish; the closure wave itself was driven by the diocese's Chapter 11 bankruptcy stemming from abuse-claim settlements, but St. Bernadette's fight was canonical, not civil.
database.appealRoute: Recourse against Bishop Michael Fisher's November 2024 merger decree → Dicastery for the Clergy, which accepted the case for full review (April 2025) and revoked the bishop's decree (announced early November 2025). No recourse to the Apostolic Signatura was needed since the parish prevailed at the Dicastery level.
database.outcomeDetail: The Vatican's Dicastery for the Clergy issued a decree revoking Bishop Michael Fisher's Road to Renewal decree that had merged St. Bernadette Parish into Ss. Peter & Paul Parish in Hamburg — the first successful appeal of the Buffalo closure wave (announced Nov. 3, 2025, reported Nov. 4-5). Advocates argued, and the ruling accepted, that the parish demonstrated sacramental and financial vitality inconsistent with the grave cause required for merger/suppression. The diocese, through Vicar for Renewal Fr. Bryan Zielenieski, said "The Diocese will, of course, abide by this ruling"; St. Bernadette remains open as a freestanding, independent parish, participating in the Family of Parishes structure alongside Ss. Peter & Paul and Nativity of Our Lord without being merged. Further Vatican reversals in the diocese (Our Lady of Peace, Holy Apostles, Our Lady of Bistrica) followed in December 2025.

database.ethnicIdentity: none/unknown
database.closureContext: Under Bishop Michael Fisher's "Road to Renewal" restructuring, the Diocese of Buffalo released its full list of recommended parish mergers in June 2024 and its official merger/closure list in September 2024, slating as many as 70+ churches for merger or closure amid a Chapter 11 bankruptcy driven by clergy sex-abuse settlements (a $150M settlement, with roughly $80M sought from parish cash accounts via special assessments). Our Lady of Peace in Clarence was decreed to merge into nearby Nativity of the Blessed Virgin Mary parish; the diocese also levied a large financial assessment on the parish for the abuse settlement.
database.communityResponse: Parishioners organized under the umbrella advocacy group Save Our Buffalo Churches (Mary Pruski), which coordinated canonical recourse for roughly 30 WNY parishes appealing to the Vatican. Our Lady of Peace parishioners mounted their own canonical appeal, documented the parish's financial health and strong participation, and joined civil litigation. Parishioner quoted in WKBW: "We stood our ground and continue to pray for everybody else in the appeal." Broader resistance pressured Bishop Fisher to rescind a 2024 policy barring parishioners from using parish spaces to organize against closures.
database.civilLitigation: Yes. Our Lady of Peace was one of five parishes (with Blessed Sacrament/Tonawanda, St. Bernadette/Orchard Park, St. Aloysius Gonzaga/Cheektowaga, St. John XXIII/West Seneca) whose parishioners — including Thomas Friedman and Timothy Creenan of Our Lady of Peace, among eight plaintiffs — sued in Erie County State Supreme Court in July 2025 seeking a TRO/preliminary injunction to block pastors from paying the diocesan abuse-settlement assessments. The suit was dismissed; New York's highest court briefly halted settlement payments in July 2025 but allowed them to proceed in September 2025, citing limits on court involvement in church governance. The assessment relief ultimately came through the canonical (Vatican) route, not the civil one.
database.appealRoute: Bishop Michael Fisher (Diocese of Buffalo) → hierarchical recourse to the Dicastery for the Clergy in Rome, coordinated through Save Our Buffalo Churches' "Canon Law Recourse process" (one of ~30 Buffalo parishes filing such appeals). The Dicastery ruled in the parish's favor; no escalation to the Apostolic Signatura was needed. The diocese retained a theoretical right to appeal the Dicastery's decision onward but no such appeal was reported.
database.outcomeDetail: The Vatican's Dicastery for the Clergy revoked Bishop Michael Fisher's Road to Renewal decree merging Our Lady of Peace (Clarence) into Nativity parish, in a decision announced by Save Our Buffalo Churches on December 8, 2025 (reported alongside Holy Apostles, Jamestown — the third and fourth confirmed Buffalo saves after St. Bernadette in November 2025). The Dicastery found "no merit" and "no substantial rationale" for the merger, citing the parish's documented financial good standing and strong parishioner participation, and provided detailed explanations of its disagreements with the bishop's decree. It also accepted the parish's appeal against the large diocesan financial assessment (levied to fund the abuse-settlement), suspending the required payment due to "grave concerns," and per advocates ruled that assessment funds and parish sale proceeds cannot finance the restructuring initiative. The parish remains open as an independent parish.
database.status2026Note: remains open as an independent parish

Long after the appeal, in 2026, eighteen volunteers incorporated a 501(c)(3), "The Church of St. Michael the Archangel Church of Tresckow, Inc." (Society of St. Michael the Archangel of Tresckow), to purchase the building from the diocese for $47,000 and keep it as an active chapel for weddings, funerals, and two annual Masses.

database.appealRoute: Bishop (Edward P. Cullen, 2008 decrees) → Congregation for the Clergy, which decided in January 2011. No onward Apostolic Signatura stage was needed or reported for this church. No protocol numbers reported in press coverage.
database.outcomeDetail: In January 2011 the Vatican's Congregation for the Clergy issued a split decree: it upheld the suppression/merger of the Tresckow parish into All Saints, McAdoo, but overturned Bishop Cullen's closure of the St. Michael church building, ruling the diocese had failed to show the "grave reason/grave cause" canon law (c. 1222) requires to withdraw a church from divine worship, and ordering the building reopened/retained as a sacred space. It was one of 8-9 Allentown churches (11 of 19 January-February 2011 Allentown/Springfield decrees were such split verdicts) whose building closures Rome reversed — an exceedingly rare reversal first reported by the AP on March 5, 2011; diocesan spokesman Matt Kerr acknowledged the buildings "may have to be used for sacred purposes." The building remained a consecrated chapel of All Saints Parish thereafter; in 2026 a lay nonprofit moved to buy it from the diocese to preserve it as an active chapel — a later disposition that does not undo the 2011 reversal of the closure decree.
