database.closureContext: Under the Diocese of Buffalo's "Road to Renewal" / Family of Parishes restructuring (led by Fr. Bryan Zielenieski, vicar for renewal), announced September 2024 amid priest shortages, declining attendance, and costs of the diocese's Chapter 11 clergy-abuse settlement, Bishop Michael W. Fisher issued a decree extinctively merging Holy Apostles Parish into St. James Parish, Jamestown, closing SS. Peter & Paul Church (508 Cherry St.) with the merger effective no later than Feb. 28, 2025; St. John Church was to remain a secondary worship site under St. James. The diocese placed the merger on hold in February 2025 once the appeal was filed.
database.communityResponse: Parishioners organized the "Save Holy Apostles Parish!" group with Samantha Scalise serving as procurator; they circulated petitions, filed a formal Remonstratio with the bishop in early 2025 (triggering the 30 "useful days" response window), vowed to "take it as far up as it will go," coordinated with the wider Save Our Buffalo Churches network (Mary Pruski), and pursued the canonical appeal to Rome. Post-Journal coverage ("Fighting Back," Feb 2025) documents the organized campaign and parishioner allegations of intimidation from diocesan clergy over petition-signing.
database.civilLitigation: None reported by the parish. Background context only: the Diocese of Buffalo was in Chapter 11 bankruptcy with a $150 million clergy sexual-abuse settlement; Holy Apostles separately appealed its assessed contribution to that settlement fund, and per the Post-Journal that appeal was accepted for examination and the assessment suspended.
database.appealRoute: Remonstratio to Bishop Michael W. Fisher (early 2025) → hierarchical recourse to the Dicastery for the Clergy, which confirmed receipt in April 2025 and suspended the merger pending review (one of ~14 Buffalo parish appeals then in Rome) → Dicastery decision December 2025 revoking the decree; further appeal to the Apostolic Signatura was available but never needed. No protocol numbers reported in press coverage; the bishop's decree exists as a scanned PDF ("Decree of the Merger of Holy Apostles Parish, Jamestown and Its Territory into St. James Parish, Jamestown") with no machine-readable text.
database.outcomeDetail: The Vatican's Dicastery for the Clergy revoked Bishop Michael W. Fisher's decree extinctively merging Holy Apostles Parish into St. James Parish, ruling on the merits that "no just cause" existed to merge the parish based on its finances, attendance, registered households, sacramental numbers, priest availability, or congregation age, and that the bishop "presented no information about how this merger will benefit our parish or any other parish." Holy Apostles remains an independent parish within Family of Parishes #4, with SS. Peter & Paul Church staying open and St. John Church continuing to operate; the Diocese of Buffalo stated it would abide by the ruling. Decided alongside Our Lady of Peace (Clarence) and reported with St. Bernadette (Orchard Park) and Sts. Peter & Paul (Hamburg) revocations in the same wave of Buffalo appeal wins.

database.ethnicIdentity: Italian (founded 1905 in Nesquehoning's "Little Italy"; rebuilt on Garibaldi Avenue in the Italian New Columbus neighborhood; parish history describes the 1950 building as "the new Italian Church")
database.closureContext: Part of Bishop Edward Cullen's 2008 diocese-wide restructuring that closed roughly 47 Allentown parishes. On July 15, 2008, Our Lady of Mount Carmel Parish, Sacred Heart Parish, and Immaculate Conception Parish in Nesquehoning were all suppressed and consolidated into the newly erected St. Francis of Assisi Parish; the Our Lady of Mount Carmel church building was closed to worship.
database.communityResponse: Parishioners filed a canonical appeal to Rome — one of 14 pending Allentown appeals — part of the multi-diocese wave of recourses coordinated with Peter Borre's Boston-based Council of Parishes network. No vigils or occupations documented for this church specifically, though contemporaneous local press describes a "bitter battle" over the Nesquehoning closures that divided the community. After the 2011 decree, the community sustained its historic St. Theresa "Shower of Roses" devotion; the church was reported declared a diocesan Shrine of Saint Therese of Lisieux (December 16, 2012) and today operates as the Shrine of Saint Therese of Lisieux with weekly Saturday Mass under St. Joseph Parish of the Panther Valley.
database.civilLitigation: None reported.
database.appealRoute: Hierarchical recourse against the bishop's 2008 suppression/closure decrees to the Vatican Congregation for the Clergy, which ruled in decrees received by parishioner groups in January 2011. No protocol numbers reported; no Apostolic Signatura stage reported for this church (the bishop retained but did not exercise the option of recourse to the Signatura, per contemporaneous reporting).
database.outcomeDetail: The Vatican Congregation for the Clergy, in a January 2011 decree, upheld the 2008 suppression of Our Lady of Mount Carmel Parish but overturned the closure of the church building itself, ordering it reopened for sacred worship. Per NCR's reporting on the parallel Allentown decrees (Minersville et al.), the Congregation found the bishop acted correctly de procedendo but failed de decernendo to establish the "graves causae" required by Canon 1222 to withdraw a church from divine worship and relegate it to profane use. Our Lady of Mount Carmel was named among seven Allentown churches receiving essentially identical January 2011 split rulings (with St. Joseph in Bethlehem, St. Canicus in Mahanoy City, Mary Queen of Peace in Pottsville, Immaculate Conception in St. Clair, St. Michael in Tresckow, and St. Roch in West Bangor, alongside St. Francis of Assisi in Minersville). Initial diocesan implementation was limited (funeral and annual/patronal Masses), but the building was returned to Catholic worship and remains in active sacred use today as the Shrine of Saint Therese of Lisieux with weekly Mass — a genuine building-reopening reversal, though not a restoration of the parish, which stayed suppressed.

database.ethnicIdentity: Croatian (personal parish founded by Croatian immigrants ca. 1910; shrine parish drawing Croatians from Canada, Pennsylvania, and Ohio)
database.closureContext: Under Bishop Michael Fisher's "Road to Renewal" restructuring (plan announced May 2024, final merger list September 2024, decrees issued roughly 18 months before the December 2025 reversal), the Diocese of Buffalo — in Chapter 11 bankruptcy and facing priest shortages and declining attendance — decreed that Our Lady of Bistrica would merge into Our Lady of the Sacred Heart and close. The decree treated the parish as a territorial parish even though it is a personal (ethnic) parish canonically erected for the Croatian community.
database.communityResponse: Parishioners appealed with coordination from the advocacy group Save Our Buffalo Churches, which shepherded canonical recourse for multiple Buffalo parishes; parish spokeswoman Mary Pruski publicly argued the diocese failed to recognize the parish's personal-parish status ("established solely by the Croatian immigrants"). After the revocation the parish returned to full operational status and began assembling documentation in anticipation of a renewed closure attempt.
database.civilLitigation: None reported specific to this parish; the diocese's broader Chapter 11 bankruptcy (abuse settlement) forms the financial backdrop, and the Vatican separately ordered review of asset assessments levied on appealing parishes.
database.appealRoute: Canonical hierarchical recourse initiated through Save Our Buffalo Churches (bishop → Dicastery for the Clergy track used by companion Buffalo parishes such as Our Lady of Peace, Clarence and Holy Apostles, Jamestown). In Bistrica's case the recourse did not require a Vatican ruling: Bishop Fisher himself revoked his decree after the procedural defect (personal parish treated as territorial) was identified. No protocol numbers reported.
database.outcomeDetail: Bishop Michael Fisher of Buffalo personally revoked his own Road to Renewal merger/closure decree against Our Lady of Bistrica, notifying parishioners by letter announced December 8, 2025, on grounds that a procedural error invalidated the decree — the diocese had processed the Croatian personal parish as if it were a territorial parish. The parish returned to full parish operational status. Caveat: the diocese reportedly said it could issue a new, corrected merger decree, so the save is provisional (validity-based revocation, not a merits ruling that the parish must stay open).

database.ethnicIdentity: Italian (church built by Italian immigrant slate-belt community, described as built by parishioners' Italian immigrant ancestors more than 100 years earlier)
database.closureContext: Closed in Bishop Edward Cullen's 2008 diocese-wide consolidation that reduced the Diocese of Allentown from 151 to 104 parishes (47 closed), many of them small ethnic parishes; St. Roch Parish was suppressed/consolidated into Our Lady of Good Counsel, Bangor, and the church building was shuttered. Successor Bishop John Barres (2009) declared himself in complete accord with Cullen's decisions and adopted them as his own.
database.communityResponse: Parishioners filed a canonical appeal to Rome — one of 14 appeals from closed Allentown parishes (a wave assisted by FutureChurch's Save Our Parish Community effort, which counts nine Allentown wins). After the decree, organizer AnnaMarie Robertone led ongoing grassroots pressure against the diocese's minimal compliance: weekly Sunday rosaries at the church grotto her uncles built, a campaign to obtain shrine status for the church, and continued demands for weekly Masses (documented 2013). No vigils/occupations reported.
database.civilLitigation: None reported.
database.appealRoute: Parishioners appealed the closure decrees of Bishop Cullen (adopted by Bishop Barres) to the Congregation for the Clergy; decrees in the Allentown cases were received by parishioner groups in January 2011. No recourse to the Apostolic Signatura reported for St. Roch. No protocol numbers reported in available sources.
database.outcomeDate: January 2011 (decrees received by parishioners; made public February-March 2011)
database.outcomeDetail: The Congregation for the Clergy overturned the bishop's decision to close St. Roch church — one of a group of shuttered Allentown churches (eight-nine reported) whose closures Rome reversed — ruling the bishop had failed to establish the "grave reason" canon 1222 requires to relegate a church and that parishioners must be allowed to use the buildings for worship; the same decrees upheld the suppression/consolidation of St. Roch Parish into Our Lady of Good Counsel, Bangor. The diocese complied minimally, opening St. Roch only for an annual (patronal feast) Mass and occasional funerals rather than the weekly Masses parishioners sought — a partial reversal (building restored to sacred use, parish not restored).

database.ethnicIdentity: none/unknown — territorial parish founded 1919 under Cardinal Dougherty for underserved north-Pottsville neighborhoods (Fishbach, Jalappa); congregation described as working-class families of mixed Irish, Italian, Slovak, and Tyrolean heritage, not a national/ethnic parish
database.closureContext: In the Diocese of Allentown's 2008 diocesan-wide restructuring (announced May-June 2008; Schuylkill County Catholics 'mourned loss of 32 churches'), Mary Queen of Peace Parish was suppressed and consolidated into Saint Patrick Parish, Pottsville; the final Mass as an independent parish was celebrated July 13, 2008, and the church building was closed to regular worship. A second closure action came on May 14, 2021, when Bishop Alfred Schlert, on the recommendation of St. Patrick's pastor Msgr. Edward O'Connor, formally closed the little-used building and offered it for sale.
database.communityResponse: Former parishioners, led by lifelong parishioner Robert Antonini, joined parishioners of neighboring closed Allentown-diocese churches (appeals from 8+ shuttered churches; 14 Allentown appeals pending at the Congregation) in petitioning the Holy See against the 2008 closures. After the 2011 decree, an August 15, 2012 Assumption Mass — the first since closure — filled the church with 300+ former parishioners. After the 2021 closure, former parishioners ('Friends of Mary Queen of Peace') fought the sale, and in October 2024 formed the Society of Mary Queen of Peace of Pottsville, Inc., a 501(c)(3) (EIN 33-1603537) that raised funds, purchased the church from the diocese, and reopened it. No vigils or occupations documented.
database.civilLitigation: None reported in any source consulted.
database.appealRoute: Hierarchical recourse from Bishop Edward Cullen's 2008 suppression/closure decrees to the Congregation for the Clergy, which ruled on the Allentown appeals in decrees handed down December 2010 - January 2011 (publicized February-March 2011); a November 2011 clarification (Republican & Herald, Nov. 4, 2011) specified six closed Allentown-diocese churches, including Mary Queen of Peace, could celebrate funeral and patronal-feast Masses. No Apostolic Signatura stage and no protocol numbers reported.
database.outcomeDetail: The Vatican Congregation for the Clergy, ruling on parishioner appeals against the Allentown diocese's 2008 closures (decrees handed down Dec 2010-Jan 2011), upheld the suppression/merger of Mary Queen of Peace Parish into St. Patrick Parish but overturned the closure of the church building, finding the bishop had failed to provide the 'grave reason' canon law (c. 1222) requires and directing that the church remain open for sacred use at the discretion of St. Patrick's pastor — it was thereafter used for an annual Assumption/patronal Mass (first on Aug 15, 2012) and funerals of former parishioners. The diocese closed the building again May 14, 2021, but it never left Catholic use: the diocese sold it to the parishioner-formed Society of Mary Queen of Peace of Pottsville, Inc., and it reopened September 22, 2025 as a Catholic chapel of the Diocese of Allentown, with Bishop Alfred Schlert decreeing the Society a private association of the Christian faithful and approving annual Masses (July 9 feast, Aug 25 dedication anniversary) plus weddings/funerals of former parishioners. Caveats: the Society's own history page loosely dates the Vatican directive '2012'; contemporaneous reporting places the decrees in Dec 2010-Jan 2011; the parish itself was never restored.
database.status2026Note: remain open for sacred use at the discretion of St

excludedAsNonReversals.ethnicIdentity: none/unknown (territorial North Hill neighborhood parish, founded 1919; no national/ethnic designation found in sources)
excludedAsNonReversals.closureContext: Part of Bishop Richard Lennon's 2009-2010 downsizing of ~50 parishes (cited shortage of priests, finances, demographics). St. Martha Parish was suppressed by merger: on December 6, 2009 it was merged with Christ the King Parish (with St. Hedwig Parish also closing, Sept. 2009) to form the new Blessed Trinity Parish. Critically, the St. Martha church building at 300 E. Tallmadge Ave. never closed — it became (and remains) the worship site of the merged Blessed Trinity Parish.
excludedAsNonReversals.communityResponse: St. Martha appeared on the Council of Parishes/attorney Peter Borré list of 13 Cleveland-diocese parishes said to have won Vatican appeals (March 2012), implying an appeal was filed on its behalf. No St. Martha-specific vigils, occupations, or petitions were documented in sources reviewed (the noted Akron sit-in was at St. John the Baptist).
excludedAsNonReversals.appealRoute: Appeal to the Congregation for the Clergy attributed to St. Martha parishioners via Boston-based Council of Parishes advocate Peter Borré, who announced on March 7-8, 2012 that 13 Cleveland parishes (including St. Martha, Akron) had won decrees. Bishop Lennon publicly stated in April 2012 that the report of 13 was incorrect and that the Congregation's decrees covered 12 parishes; St. Martha was not among them. No Apostolic Signatura stage and no protocol numbers reported for St. Martha.
excludedAsNonReversals.outcomeDetail: Not a reversal. On April 17, 2012 Bishop Lennon announced he would reopen the 12 parishes whose March 2012 Congregation for the Clergy decrees voided his closings; the only Akron parishes among them were St. John the Baptist and St. Mary. St. Martha — the disputed 13th name on attorney Peter Borré's list — was not restored: Lennon stated the 13-parish count was incorrect. The 2009 merger of St. Martha into Blessed Trinity Parish stands today; the Diocese of Cleveland directory and Blessed Trinity's own history page list Blessed Trinity Parish (founded Dec. 6, 2009) worshipping at St. Martha Church, with no mention of any 2012 restoration. Masses held today at the St. Martha building are Blessed Trinity Parish Masses; the building itself never closed.
excludedAsNonReversals.exclusionReason: The claim conflates the St. Martha church building (never closed — it became the merged Blessed Trinity Parish's worship site in Dec. 2009) with St. Martha Parish (suppressed by merger, never restored). St. Martha was not among the 12 parishes whose closings the Congregation for the Clergy decrees voided and which Lennon reopened in 2012; the "13 parishes" figure including St. Martha came from the appellants' attorney and was publicly corrected by the diocese to 12. No source documents a St. Martha reopening in 2012 or a restored independent St. Martha Parish; the parish remains merged into Blessed Trinity today.

excludedAsNonReversals.parish: St. Adalbert Parish / St. Adalbert Basilica
excludedAsNonReversals.ethnicIdentity: Polish (founded 1886 by Polish immigrants; 'Bazylika Swietego Wojciecha'; proclaimed a basilica in 1907, claimed as America's first)
excludedAsNonReversals.closureDate: July 2007 (Bishop Kmiec merger decision; last Mass initially scheduled Nov 25, 2007); merger executed Sept 18, 2011; successor St. John Kanty Parish ordered closed/merged May 2025
excludedAsNonReversals.closureContext: 2007 'Journey in Faith' diocesan downsizing under Bishop Edward Kmiec: St. Adalbert Parish ordered merged into St. John Kanty Parish. 2024-25 'Road to Renewal' under Bishop Michael Fisher: the successor St. John Kanty Parish (of which St. Adalbert is a secondary worship site/oratory) ordered merged into St. Stanislaus; final Mass May 2025 and church locked.
excludedAsNonReversals.communityResponse: 2007-2012: 'Save St. Adalbert' committee filed successive Vatican appeals that delayed execution about four years. 2025: parishioners held prayer vigils outside the locked St. John Kanty church and petitioned the Roman Curia with help from Save Our Buffalo Churches (Mary Pruski) and the St. Joseph Foundation; leaders Ed Zielinski and Matthew Zawisky received the Vatican letters. Parishioners also secured City of Buffalo landmark status for the basilica and are pursuing verification of its minor-basilica title.
excludedAsNonReversals.civilLitigation: None reported; preservation efforts proceeded via municipal landmark designation, not lawsuits.
excludedAsNonReversals.appealRoute: Phase 1: Bishop Edward Kmiec decree (July 2007) → Congregation for the Clergy (rejected appeal; hierarchical recourse upheld merger, reported Feb 2008 and 2011) → Apostolic Signatura (upheld, ruling that Kmiec properly followed canon law; reported 2012). Phase 2: petition/recourse against Bishop Michael Fisher's Road to Renewal merger of St. John Kanty into St. Stanislaus → Dicastery for the Clergy; prefect's letter of Sept 26, 2025 suspended the closure for approximately 90 days pending further examination; final ruling still pending as of April 2026 reporting.
excludedAsNonReversals.outcomeDetail: No final reversal. The 2007-2011 canonical appeals failed: the Congregation for the Clergy and then the Apostolic Signatura upheld Bishop Kmiec's merger of St. Adalbert into St. John Kanty; the parish was suppressed in September 2011 and the building retained only as an oratory with about four Masses a year — an arrangement within the upheld decree, not a reversal of it. On Sept 26, 2025 the Dicastery for the Clergy prefect suspended (for ~90 days, pending examination) the Road to Renewal closure of the successor St. John Kanty Parish after a parishioner petition; that suspension formally concerns St. John Kanty, does not name St. Adalbert in coverage of the letters, and remained an unresolved interim measure as of the latest reporting (April 2026). The Vatican also reported in 2025 that no documentation attests St. Adalbert's minor-basilica title.
excludedAsNonReversals.exclusionReason: Failed appeal plus unresolved interim action: the 2007-2011 merger was upheld at both the Congregation for the Clergy and the Apostolic Signatura and the parish was suppressed (delays ended in closure anyway); the church surviving as an occasional-use oratory was part of the upheld outcome, not a revocation. The Sept 26, 2025 Dicastery suspension is a temporary, still-pending measure directed at the successor St. John Kanty Parish's merger, not a decree invalidation or reopening of St. Adalbert, and no final Vatican ruling had issued as of the latest available reporting.

leadsNotYetInvestigated: St. Stanislaus Kostka, Adams — After Bishop Timothy McDonnell suppressed the Polish national parish and closed its church on Jan 1, 2009 (merged into Pope John Paul the Great Parish), parishioners held a 1,150-day round-the-clock vigil and won at the Vatican: in February 2011 the Congregation for the Clergy upheld the parish suppression but overturned the church closure for lack of grave cause, in November 2011 the Vatican barred the diocese from selling the building, and the church reopened on Palm Sunday, April 1, 2012 — it remains open today as a mission church of St. John Paul II Parish.

Immaculate Conception, Indian Orchard (Springfield) — Bishop Timothy McDonnell scheduled the Polish national parish to close in 2009, granted an extension to June 2010 after parishioners organized, and on Sept 18, 2010 accepted the parish's long-term viability plan and reversed his own closure decision — the parish remains open today with no Vatican intervention needed.

St. George, Chicopee — In February 2011 the Vatican's Congregation for the Clergy upheld Bishop McDonnell's 2009 merger of the French-Canadian-founded parish (est. 1893 as St. Joachim) into Holy Name of Jesus but ruled he had not shown sufficiently grave reason to close the church building itself and said it should reopen for worship, and in November 2011 the Vatican barred the diocese from selling it — though the merged parish never restored regular Masses there and the building's long-term disposition remains contested by the Friends of St. George Church.

St. Patrick, Chicopee (Chicopee Falls) — In the same February 2011 decrees, the Congregation for the Clergy upheld the parish's 2009 merger into Holy Name of Jesus but overturned Bishop McDonnell's closure of the church building for lack of grave cause, and in 2012 the church reopened for a weekly Mass under Holy Name Parish — a successful canonical appeal, though Holy Name later held a final Mass there in a subsequent closure round and the parish itself closed in June 2023 (verify whether later re-closure disqualifies it).

St. Anne (St. Anne Shrine), Fall River — After the diocese suppressed the historic French-Canadian St. Anne's Parish and closed its landmark church in November 2018 over repair costs, the lay St. Anne's Preservation Society organized, negotiated a 10-year $1-per-year lease from the diocese, and reopened the church as St. Anne Shrine on July 4, 2019, with Bishop Edgar da Cunha celebrating the first Mass on the July 26 feast of St. Anne — the building remains an active Catholic shrine.

St. Casimir Parish, Cleveland — Lithuanian personal parish closed by Bishop Richard Lennon in November 2009; the Congregation for the Clergy's March 2012 decrees found the closure violated canon law and procedure (one of 13 Cleveland parishes overturned), and the parish reopened in July 2012 and remains active today.

St. James Parish, Lakewood — One of the 13 Diocese of Cleveland parishes whose 2010 closure by Bishop Lennon the Congregation for the Clergy overturned in March 2012 for failure to follow canonical procedure, after a parishioner-led appeal to Rome; the church reopened in 2012.

St. Wendelin Parish, Cleveland — Slovak-heritage parish closed in 2010 and reopened in 2012 after the Congregation for the Clergy's March 2012 decrees vacated Bishop Lennon's closings of 13 Cleveland-diocese parishes on procedural and canon-law grounds.

St. Stanislaus Kostka Church, Adams — Historic Polish church closed in December 2008; after a 1,150-day round-the-clock parishioner vigil and canonical recourse, the Congregation for the Clergy upheld the parish merger but overturned the closure of the church building, which reopened for worship on Palm Sunday, April 1, 2012 and now serves as a mission church of St. John Paul II Parish.

Immaculate Conception Church, Wellsville — In 2012 the Congregation for the Clergy partially upheld parishioners' appeal and blocked the bishop's closing of Immaculate Conception Church, ruling that even when parishes are merged the church building must remain available for worship absent a compelling reason.

Holy Apostles Parish, Jamestown — In December 2025 the Dicastery for the Clergy upheld the parish's canonical recourse against Bishop Fisher's decree merging it with St. James Parish in Jamestown, overturning the merger so Holy Apostles will not close.

St. Mary Church, East Arcade — On January 14, 2026 the Dicastery for the Clergy overturned Bishop Fisher's February 4, 2025 decree closing this rural Wyoming County church, ruling he failed to demonstrate the grave cause canon law requires, reinstating the parish after about 90 parishioners appealed.

St. Angela Merici Parish, Florissant — The Dicastery for the Clergy, ruling on the parish's recourse against Archbishop Mitchell Rozanski's All Things New restructuring, found no just cause to merge St. Angela Merici with St. Norbert and Holy Name of Jesus parishes and declined to sustain the decree, so the parish stayed open.

St. Richard Parish, Creve Coeur — The Dicastery for the Clergy refused to uphold Archbishop Rozanski's All Things New decree consolidating St. Richard, finding no just cause given the parish's financial and demographic stability, one of three St. Louis merger appeals the Vatican sustained, so the parish remained open.

St. Angela Merici Parish (with St. Norbert and Holy Name of Jesus), Florissant — In May 2024 the Dicastery for the Clergy reversed the 'All Things New' plan to merge St. Angela Merici with St. Norbert and Holy Name of Jesus parishes, finding no just cause, so all three north St. Louis County parishes remain separate.

St. Martin of Tours Parish, Lemay (St. Louis County) — In 2024 the Dicastery for the Clergy found no just cause for the 'All Things New' decree subsuming St. Martin of Tours into St. Mark Parish, reversing the merger so both remain separate parish communities.

St. Casimir Parish (Polish; named for the patron saint of Poland and Lithuania), Cleveland — Closed by Bishop Richard Lennon in November 2009, the Polish east-side parish won its appeal when the Vatican's Congregation for the Clergy on March 1, 2012 overturned the suppression (one of 13 Cleveland parishes ordered restored in an unprecedented mass reversal), and after 2.5 years of weekly parishioner vigils the church reopened July 15, 2012.

Our Lady of Good Counsel Church, New Orleans — Closed in the 2008 post-Katrina consolidation and occupied by protesting parishioners until a January 2009 police eviction, the church was reopened by Archbishop Gregory Aymond (announced December 11, 2015; first Mass January 2, 2016) as a shrine housing the Center of Jesus the Lord charismatic community with regular Masses restored.

St. Henry Church (founded 1855, historically German), New Orleans — Closed in the post-Katrina consolidation (parishioners occupied the Uptown church until January 2009 and pursued Vatican recourse), it was reopened by Archbishop Gregory Aymond for daily Mass beginning June 15, 2012 as a worship site of Good Shepherd Parish.

St. Aloysius (Carthagena), St. Bernard (Burkettsville), St. Francis (Cranberry Prairie) and St. Wendelin parishes, Mercer County (St. Henry area) — PENDING, verify before use: after Archbishop Dennis Schnurr's October 1, 2023 Beacons of Light decree amalgamating these four Land of the Cross-Tipped Churches parishes into St. Henry Parish, the Vatican's Dicastery for the Clergy accepted the parishioners' hierarchical recourse (canon lawyer Philip Gray), suspending canonical changes to the churches while the appeal is decided; no final reversal documented yet.

Annunciation of the Blessed Virgin Mary (Lithuanian national parish since 1914) — now Our Lady of Mount Carmel–Annunciation Parish, Williamsburg, Brooklyn, New York City — Rather than closing the shrinking Lithuanian national parish, the Diocese of Brooklyn merged it into neighboring Our Lady of Mount Carmel effective June 1, 2018 with the 1863 church kept open, financed a yearlong multi-million-dollar restoration by leasing the former parish school, and Bishop Nicholas DiMarzio rededicated the church on March 24, 2019, preserving New York City's only weekly Lithuanian-language liturgy (which also absorbed Maspeth's relocated Lithuanian Mass in September 2025) — flag for verification: coverage frames the merger as the alternative to closure, but no formal closure decree or canonical appeal is documented.

Immaculate Conception (Lithuanian), East St. Louis — The 1956 Lithuanian folk-art church outlasted the collapse of Catholic East St. Louis — 11 of the area's 13 Catholic churches closed by 2018 — and although the Diocese of Belleville's own closed-parishes list records Immaculate Conception parish as closed in 2014, the diocese's current directory still lists the building as an active chapel with continuing Masses and an attending congregation — flag for verification: determine whether a proposed closure was fought and cancelled (a reversal) or the parish was quietly downgraded to chapel status (not one).

St. Peter Lithuanian Parish, South Boston (Boston) — Lithuanian national parish placed on the May 25, 2004 closing list; parishioners formed the Friends of St. Peter Lithuanian Parish and built a protest 'Hill of Crosses' modeled on Siauliai's, and Archbishop O'Malley granted the parish a reprieve in June 2005 (prioritizing immigrant-serving parishes) - the closure was never carried out and the parish remains open today.

Blessed Kateri Tekakwitha (now St. Kateri Tekakwitha), Plymouth — Slated to close in the May 2004 plan; parishioners gave the archdiocese's Meade-Eisner review committee new evidence of Plymouth's population growth, the committee unanimously recommended keeping it open, and Archbishop O'Malley granted the reprieve on Dec. 14, 2004 - his first outright reversal of a 2004 closure; the church is still in use, since a June 2022 merger as a worship site of Mary Queen of Martyrs Parish.

St. Mary of the Angels, Roxbury (Egleston Square), Boston — Small multicultural (English/Spanish-speaking) parish that found itself on the May 2004 suppression list; parishioners organized and successfully argued the parish was essential to the Egleston Square community, and the archdiocese dropped the closure - the parish never closed and remains open in the Jamaica Plain/Roxbury collaborative.

St. Bernard, West Newton (Newton) — Suppression announced in 2004; parishioners kept vigil inside the church to force re-examination, and after Meade-Eisner committee review the archdiocese cancelled the suppression, instead merging St. Bernard with Corpus Christi (Auburndale) on July 1, 2006 with BOTH churches remaining open as worship sites of the new Corpus Christi-St. Bernard Parish.

Mary Immaculate of Lourdes, Newton (Newton Upper Falls) — Historic parish placed on the 2004 closure list; O'Malley ordered a re-evaluation (announced Dec. 14, 2004, with a new administrator) and in 2006 reversed the closure, keeping it open as a non-territorial parish dedicated to the Traditional Latin Mass community - it remains open today.

St. Susanna, Dedham — Announced for suppression on May 25, 2004; pastor Fr. Stephen Josoma immediately vowed appeal and the roughly 800-family parish organized against the closure, which was never executed - the parish remains open and active today.

Holy Cross Parish, Buffalo — The diocese reversed its September 2024 Road to Renewal final-plan decision to close the historic West Side church (founded as an Italian parish, now serving Rwandan, African and Hispanic communities), announcing in February 2025 after the parish's challenge and counterproposal review that Holy Cross would remain open, placed on a financial watch list.

St. Maximilian Kolbe Parish (East Pembroke worship site), East Pembroke — Bishop Michael Fisher reversed the Road to Renewal draft recommendation to close the East Pembroke church after reviewing the parish's counterproposal, announcing on September 9-10, 2024 that St. Maximilian Kolbe would remain open (with Ascension Parish in Batavia merged into Resurrection instead).

St. Hedwig Parish, Dunkirk — Bishop Edward Kmiec reversed the Journey in Faith and Grace regional recommendation to close this Polish parish's church weeks after the plan was unveiled, allowing St. Hedwig to remain open following renewed consultation with the diocesan strategic planning commission (it later became part of Dunkirk's merged Blessed Mary Angela Parish).

St. Joseph Parish, Fredonia — Bishop Edward Kmiec reversed the Journey in Faith and Grace recommendation to close St. Joseph in Fredonia weeks after it was announced, and the church remained open as an independent parish in the northern Chautauqua County restructuring.

St. Barbara Parish (Polish, founded 1909), Chicago (Bridgeport) — A November 2018 Renew My Church scenario slated Polish-founded St. Barbara for closure, but after parishioner pushback Cardinal Blase Cupich's final January 2019 decision cancelled the closure, uniting St. Barbara with St. Therese Chinese Catholic Mission effective July 1, 2019 with both churches remaining open as active worship sites.

Shrine of Christ the King (former St. Clara / St. Gelasius, German-founded), Chicago (Woodlawn) — After an October 2015 fire the Archdiocese of Chicago announced demolition in January 2016, but the Coalition to Save the Shrine raised over $650,000 in weeks and in March 2016 the archdiocese reversed course and deeded the landmark church to the Institute of Christ the King Sovereign Priest, which restored it for Catholic worship (the same building had already been saved from archdiocesan demolition plans in 2003-2004 when Cardinal George gave it to the Institute).

St. Joseph's Pro-Cathedral (historically Polish), Camden — Slated for merger in Bishop Joseph Galante's April 2008 reorganization (70 to 66 parishes), the historically Polish pro-cathedral was kept as a stand-alone parish when Galante scaled back the plan on August 27, 2008 after organized parishioner opposition.

St. Katharine Drexel Parish, Egg Harbor City — Originally ordered to merge with St. Vincent de Paul in Mays Landing under the Diocese of Camden's April 2008 consolidation plan, the merger was cancelled in Bishop Galante's revised August 2008 plan and the parish remained stand-alone.

Sweetest Heart of Mary (Polish), Detroit — NEEDS VERIFICATION: the landmark Polish church was repeatedly threatened in archdiocesan downsizings (Cardinal Edmund Szoka's 1988-89 plan, in which a study group's 43 proposed closures were softened to 31 before implementation, and the 2011-12 restructuring) but was never closed after parishioner resistance, remaining open for regular Masses as part of Mother of Divine Mercy Parish formed in 2013 - the specific reprieve decision should be confirmed before database inclusion.

Transfiguration (Lithuanian) Church — Transfiguration–St. Stanislaus Kostka Parish, Maspeth, Queens, New York City — Diocese officials told parishioners on March 9, 2025 that the 1908-founded Lithuanian parish's Jonas Mulokas-designed church (built 1962 with Lithuanian-American donations) would close and the property might be sold, but after a campaign by Knights of Lithuania Council 110 (840+-signature petition, landmark-designation push), formal appeals from the Lithuanian Consulate General in New York and the Lithuanian Bishops' Conference, and heavy Lithuanian press coverage, Bishop Robert Brennan announced in a letter read at Masses on August 23-24, 2025 that Transfiguration Church will not be closed or sold (weekend Masses continue; only part of the surrounding property may be sold), and Lithuanian President Gitanas Nauseda visited the church that fall — note the weekly Lithuanian Mass itself was relocated to Annunciation in Williamsburg on September 1, 2025.
